NSI Daily Market Report 22/12/2025
Hi Deepesh
We have opened up in line with last few days rises.
The US Energy Information Administration (EIA) has rewritten its December Short-Term Energy Outlook this week, saying that OPEC\'s crude oil production capacity will be more than previously expected. The agency\'s upward revision now estimates that OPEC\'s effective production capacity in 2024 was higher by roughly 220,000 barrels per day, in 2025 by 370,000 barrels per day, and 310,000 barrels per day in 2026, compared to its assessments from earlier this month. The EIA has refined the two concepts that it uses in order to assess supply risk: the first being maximum sustainable capacity and the second is effective production capacity. Maximum sustainable capacity is a producer\'s theoretical upper limit that they could reach within a year if everything runs smoothly. Whereas, effective capacity is the realistic amount of oil that could be brought online within 90 days and will be able to be sustained without damaging fields or infrastructure. The EIA uses effective production capacity figures to judge how much oil is actually available in order to respond to market shocks; the change in its key assumption over the global oil market came from rethinking what "capacity" actually means, rather than from new drilling or surprise barrels. The agency had concluded that OPEC\'s buffer is larger than previously assumed after tightening those definitions and reassessing the disruptions that’s occurred over the year - since OPEC\'s actual production estimates were mostly unchanged, the revisions were brought almost directly into higher spare capacity estimates. Oil prices react strongly to refinery outages, sanctions, wars, or severe weather (such as hurricanes) which spare capacity acts as a shock absorber for oil markets. Over the past few years, oil prices have been bashed in every direction, particularly in 2025 after Trump came into office. The war between Israel and Palestine, as well as the Russia-Ukraine conflict has created a lot of volatility in the market, as energy infrastructures have been heavy targets during attacks, on top of the heavy number of tariffs and sanctions that has been implemented, which a large portion of them are linked to these wars, as the United States in particular has been particularly aggressive in its pressure campaigns. Aside from that, geopolitical tensions, have also caused oil prices to fluctuate and created a lot of uncertainty in the markets. It can be determined that the US has significantly influenced oil prices and the overall market in 2025, to name a few: its trade war with China on top of disruptions in trading relations with Canada and Venezuela earlier this year - which tensions with Venezuela appear to be escalating within the past couple weeks after the US had seized a tanker offshore the South American county. Also to note is the US sanctions on Russia and on nations that have continued purchasing its energy commodities have boosted geopolitical tensions as well as change global trading dynamics, particularly affected India and China. Considering all this, spare capacity has become crucial in order to maintain energy security as high geopolitical risk carries less pricing power. Therefore, The EIA\'s latest update suggests that supply is less fragile in the market than many traders have believed. However, since OPEC has justified its production discipline this month and for the first few months of 2026 on the narrative of tight capacity, the EIA\'s reassessment isnt a bullish message, causing OPEC\'s message to be weakened, though it hasn’t completely blown up their argument.
Russia is heading into a sharp drop in oil tax revenue into the start of 2026. In January, it\'s tax proceeds from crude oil production could fall to roughly $4.7 billion, according to Reuters calculations released on Friday, which would be the lowest monthly take since late 2022. This would be a 16% drop from December and would mark an over 50% decrease compared with January 2024. Oil prices had been a major factor towards the drop in Russia\'s revenues as its export prices are cheaper this month compared to November, with prices averaging down by roughly 12%, resulting in the mineral extraction tax that\'s tied to those prices to mechanically reduce. On Friday 19th December, Russia\'s main export grade Urals was selling below $35 per barrel, according to Argus - the value of export earning had reduced once concerted into local currency since the stronger rouble had compounded the lower prices. The mineral extraction tax is due at the end of every month, reflecting the production during the previous month. The effective tax rate is estimated at approximately 14,266 roubles per tonne in December, nearly 20% lower than November and more than 50% below the level seen a year earlier, the rates similar to when the EU\'s embargo on Russian oil had formally kicked in December 2022. Furthermore, Reuters calculations earlier this month showed Russia’s combined oil and gas revenues has nearly halved year on year in December, falling to levels that had been last seen in 2020 during the demand crash in the pandemic-era. The combination of lower prices, a stronger currency, and increasingly discounts on Urals crude - that\'s been widened by the latest US sanctions on Rosneft and Lukoil) have all contributed in Russia\'s dwindling cash flow. Additionally, Russia has seen its refining margins to also weaken which has caused its tax receipts to be dragged down from crude along with oil products. Considering that the Russian government continues to heavily rely on oil revenues in order to fund military spending for its war with Ukraine and social obligations, the timing comes at a crucial state. With the latest Western sanctions on Russia\'s top oil majors, Rosneft and Lukoil, sanctions targeting its shadow fleet as well as on other entities and individuals that have enabled Russian oil to continue flowing in the markets, the upcoming losses in its budget for its war may be enough incentive to pressure Putin into agreeing to the latest peace deal negotiations. Notably, the talks are being brokered by the US and all the mentioned factors above have boosted optimism that a ceasefire will be made, particularly after Trump\'s latest threat to put additional sanctions on Russia if it doesn’t agree to a deal. More sanctions could reduce Russia\'s oil flows further and cut down its revenues even more, meaning that even if Putin doesn’t agree to a peace deal, its budget for war may eventually run too low to be able to keep up with Ukraine. The strain on Russia\'s oil revenues and its overall energy industry has reflected in its policy debates as Moscow is already considering the idea of tax relief for Gazprom in attempt to cushion the collapse in pipeline gas exports to Europe in preparation of higher taxes within the energy sector potentially causing some exports to be offset. Russia\'s oil and gas continues to supply around a quarter of Russia’s budget, but the fact that Russia is considering this kind of fiscal reshuffling strongly suggests that the cushion is thinning and that it is becoming more desperate to maintain its energy security whilst also trying to fund its conflict with Ukraine. Russia can possibly absorb the blow for now, but it could end up in a very volatile situation in 2026 if oil prices stay soft and sanctions continue to disrupt its oil flows. However, even if this is the breaking point for Russia into agreeing to the latest peace deal, market sentiment is still down as this could lead to the sanctions being lifted, meaning that Russian oil will be able to flood the market amid concerns of an oversupply and bearish warnings of a global glut next year.
